title: Existence, Uniqueness, and Geometry
subtitle: The geometric and theoretical heart of first-order theory: visual fields, the phase plane, and why solutions exist and are unique.
description: The geometric and theoretical heart of first-order theory: visual fields, the phase plane, and why solutions exist and are unique.

atAGlance.title: Unit 3 at a Glance: Core Sub-Topics
atAGlance.summary: This unit is the geometric and theoretical heart of first-order theory. It trades formula-hunting for understanding: how to read an equation as a picture, and why a solution should exist and be the only one.
bullets: \textbf{Slope Fields and Vector Fields} --- the equation $y' = f(x,y)$ as a field of tiny tangent segments, traced by integral curves.
\textbf{The Phase Plane and Phase Portraits} --- states, trajectories, equilibria, and nullclines for a system of equations.
\textbf{Existence and Uniqueness} --- the two continuity hypotheses that guarantee exactly one solution through a point.
\textbf{Fixed Points and Iteration} --- solving $g(x)=x$ by repeated application, and when the iteration is attracting.
\textbf{The Banach Fixed Point Theorem} --- a contraction on a complete space has one fixed point, reached from any start.
\textbf{Picard Iteration} --- recasting an IVP as an integral equation and converging to its solution.

cheatSubtitle: Condensed Cheat Sheet: Existence, Uniqueness, and Geometry

cheatTakeaway.title: Unit 3 Key Takeaway
cheatTakeaway.body:
This unit reads differential equations as \textcolor{accentorange}{geometry and
guarantees} rather than formulas. A \textcolor{accentorange}{slope field} turns
$y'=f(x,y)$ into a picture its integral curves follow; the
\textcolor{accentorange}{phase plane} does the same for systems. The
\textcolor{accentorange}{existence and uniqueness theorem} ($f$ and
$\partial f/\partial y$ continuous) says exactly one curve threads each point,
and \textcolor{accentorange}{Picard iteration via the Banach contraction
principle} is the machine that proves it.

modules.title: Slope Fields and Vector Fields
modules.topics: A first order equation $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x,y)$ hands you a \emph{number} at every point of the plane, and a derivative is a slope. So the equation is secretly a picture: at each point plant a short segment whose slope is $f(x,y)$. A \emph{solution} is any curve that stays tangent to those segments everywhere it goes.

formulas.title: Slope Field, Integral Curves, and Isoclines
formulas.body:
At each point $(x,y)$ the field draws a segment of slope $f(x,y)$.
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=2pt]
    \item An \textbf{integral curve} is a solution that is tangent to the field
          at every point it crosses.
    \item An \textbf{isocline} is the curve $f(x,y) = c$ of all points sharing
          one slope $c$; the isocline $f(x,y)=0$ marks the flat segments.
\end{itemize}

examples.title: Worked Example 3.1: The field of $\frac{dy}{dx}=x+y$
examples.setup:
\textbf{Step 1.} Read off a slope by substituting coordinates. At $(1,2)$:
\[ f(1,2) = 1 + 2 = 3. \]
\textbf{Step 2.} Find the flat isocline by setting $f=0$:
\[ x + y = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; y = -x. \]
Along $y=-x$ every segment is horizontal; integral curves cross it with zero
slope.

modules.title: The Phase Plane and Phase Portraits
modules.topics: For a \emph{system} $x' = f(x,y)$, $y' = g(x,y)$, the plane no longer carries time on an axis --- each point is a complete \emph{state}, and as $t$ runs the state point sweeps out a \emph{trajectory}. A \emph{phase portrait} is a representative bundle of trajectories that reveals the global flow.

formulas.title: Equilibria, Nullclines, and Linear Classification
formulas.body:
An \textbf{equilibrium} satisfies $x'=0$ and $y'=0$ simultaneously. The
$x$-nullcline is $\{x'=0\}$ and the $y$-nullcline is $\{y'=0\}$. For a linear
system the eigenvalues of the coefficient matrix classify the origin:
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\text{real, same sign} & \text{node (source if } +,\ \text{sink if } -)\\
\text{real, opposite signs} & \text{saddle}\\
\text{complex, nonzero real part} & \text{spiral}\\
\text{purely imaginary} & \text{center (closed orbits)}
\end{array}
\]

examples.title: Worked Example 3.2: Classify $x'=y,\ y'=-x$
examples.setup:
\textbf{Step 1.} Set both right sides to zero for the equilibrium:
\[ y = 0 \text{ and } -x = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; (0,0). \]
\textbf{Step 2.} The coefficient matrix
$\left(\begin{smallmatrix}0&1\\-1&0\end{smallmatrix}\right)$
has eigenvalues $\pm i$ --- purely imaginary.
\[ \text{Purely imaginary} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{a center: closed orbits around the origin.} \]

modules.title: Existence and Uniqueness of Solutions
modules.topics: Before solving, ask whether a solution even exists and whether it is the \emph{only} one. The Big Theorem answers both with two continuity hypotheses --- one for each conclusion.

formulas.title: The Existence and Uniqueness Theorem

examples.title: Worked Example 3.3: Where uniqueness fails for $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{x}{y-2}$
examples.setup:
\textbf{Step 1.} Identify where $f$ and $\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}$ lose
continuity --- watch the denominator:
\[ f(x,y) = \frac{x}{y-2} \text{ is undefined when } y - 2 = 0. \]
\textbf{Step 2.} Conclude:
\[ \text{The theorem fails along the line } y = 2. \]
Off that line both $f$ and $\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = -\frac{x}{(y-2)^2}$
are continuous, so a unique solution threads each such point.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Existence Is Not Uniqueness
warning.body:
A continuous $f$ with a \emph{discontinuous} $\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}$
still has a solution --- just maybe more than one. For $y' = \sqrt{y}$,
$y(0)=0$, the derivative $\frac{1}{2\sqrt{y}}$ blows up at $y=0$, and indeed
both $y=0$ and $y=\frac{x^2}{4}$ pass through the origin. The guarantee is also
only \emph{local}: never read it as a promise for all $x$.

modules.title: Fixed Points and Iteration
modules.topics: A \emph{fixed point} of $g$ is a value left unmoved by the map: $g(x^*)=x^*$. Geometrically it is where $y=g(x)$ meets the line $y=x$. Feeding outputs back as inputs --- $x_{n+1}=g(x_n)$ --- either pulls toward such a point or pushes away.

formulas.title: Fixed Point and Stability Test
formulas.body:
\[ x^* \text{ is a fixed point} \iff g(x^*) = x^*. \]
The iteration $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ is \textbf{attracting} (stable) at $x^*$ when
\[ |g'(x^*)| < 1, \qquad \text{and \textbf{repelling} when } |g'(x^*)| > 1. \]

examples.title: Worked Example 3.4: Fixed points of $g(x)=x^{2}$
examples.setup:
\textbf{Step 1.} Solve $g(x)=x$:
\[ x^2 = x \;\Longrightarrow\; x(x-1)=0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x=0 \text{ or } x=1. \]
\textbf{Step 2.} Test stability with $|g'(x)| = |2x|$:
\[ |g'(0)| = 0 < 1 \ (\text{attracting}), \qquad |g'(1)| = 2 > 1 \ (\text{repelling}). \]

modules.title: The Banach Fixed Point Theorem
modules.topics: The deepest reason iteration converges is \emph{contraction}: a map that shrinks the gap between any two points. On a complete space such a map cannot help but funnel everything to a single point.

formulas.title: Contraction Mapping and Banach's Conclusion
formulas.body:
$g$ is a \textbf{contraction} with constant $0 \le k < 1$ if
\[ |g(x) - g(y)| \le k\,|x - y| \quad \text{for all } x,y. \]
On a \textbf{complete} space mapped into itself, Banach guarantees a
\textbf{unique} fixed point $x^*$, reached by iteration from \emph{any} start,
with the error bound
\[ |x_n - x^*| \le \frac{k^n}{1-k}\,|x_1 - x_0|. \]

examples.title: {Worked Example 3.5: $g(x)=\cos x$ is a contraction on $[0,1]$}
examples.setup:
\textbf{Step 1.} A bound $|g'(x)| \le k < 1$ makes $g$ a contraction. Here
\[ |g'(x)| = |\sin x|, \quad \text{increasing on } [0,1]. \]
\textbf{Step 2.} Its largest value on the interval is at $x=1$:
\[ |\sin x| \le \sin 1 \approx 0.841 < 1. \]
So $g$ is a contraction, and iterating cosine converges to its unique fixed
point (the Dottie number, $\approx 0.739$) from any start.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: $k=1$ Is Not Enough
warning.body:
The constant must be \emph{strictly} below one. With $k=1$ distances need not
shrink, so the iterates may never converge --- e.g. $g(x)=x+1$ satisfies
$|g(x)-g(y)|=|x-y|$ ($k=1$) and has \emph{no} fixed point at all.

modules.title: Picard Iteration
modules.topics: Picard iteration is Banach's principle in action on differential equations. First the IVP is recast as an \emph{integral equation}; then repeated substitution builds the solution as a limit.

formulas.title: Integral Equation and the Picard Scheme
formulas.body:
The IVP $y' = f(x,y)$, $y(x_0)=y_0$ is equivalent to
\[ y(x) = y_0 + \int_{x_0}^{x} f\big(t, y(t)\big)\,dt. \]
Iterate from $\varphi_0 = y_0$:
\[ \varphi_{n+1}(x) = y_0 + \int_{x_0}^{x} f\big(t, \varphi_n(t)\big)\,dt. \]
\textbf{Picard--Lindel\"of:} if $f$ is continuous and \textbf{Lipschitz} in $y$
($|f(x,y_1)-f(x,y_2)| \le L\,|y_1-y_2|$), the operator is a contraction, so the
solution exists and is unique on some interval.

examples.title: Worked Example 3.6: Picard iterates for $y'=y,\ y(0)=1$
examples.setup:
\textbf{Step 1.} Start at $\varphi_0 = 1$ and integrate:
\[ \varphi_1 = 1 + \int_0^x 1\,dt = 1 + x. \]
\textbf{Step 2.} Feed it back:
\[ \varphi_2 = 1 + \int_0^x (1+t)\,dt = 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2}. \]
\textbf{Step 3.} The iterates are the partial sums of a familiar series:
\[ \varphi_n \to 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2} + \cdots = e^{x}, \]
which is exactly the true solution.

practice.subtitle: Practice Set: Problems and Complete Solutions
practice.intro: Work the following ten problems in order. They climb from reading a slope field, through the phase plane and the existence--uniqueness test, into fixed points, contraction mappings, and a full Picard iteration. Solutions follow in Part III.
problems: For the slope field of $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x + y$, find the slope at the point $(1,2)$ and the isocline along which every segment is horizontal.
For $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = y^{2} - x$, find the curve along which every field segment has slope $2$.
For $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -\dfrac{x}{y}$, find the slope of the segment at $(3,4)$, and describe the shape of the integral curves.
For the system $x' = y$, $y' = -x$, locate the equilibrium and classify it using the eigenvalues of its coefficient matrix.
For the linear system $x' = 2x$, $y' = 3y$, write the eigenvalues of the coefficient matrix and classify the origin.
For $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{x}{y - 2}$, determine where the existence and uniqueness theorem fails to guarantee a unique solution.
Show that the initial value problem $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \sqrt{y}$, $y(0) = 0$ has more than one solution, and explain which hypothesis of the theorem fails.
Find every fixed point of $g(x) = x^{2}$ and classify each as attracting or repelling.
Show that $g(x) = \cos x$ is a contraction on $[0,1]$, and state what the Banach fixed point theorem then concludes.

\textbf{(Capstone)} For $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = y$ with $y(0) = 1$, carry out Picard iteration from $\varphi_0 = 1$: compute $\varphi_1$, $\varphi_2$, $\varphi_3$, and identify the function the iterates converge to.
solutions:
\noindent\textbf{Solution 1.}\quad The slope is just $f$ evaluated at the point.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{f(1,2) = 1 + 2 = 3.} \]
The horizontal segments lie on the isocline $f = 0$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{x + y = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; y = -x.} \]

\noindent\textbf{Solution 2.}\quad Set $f$ equal to the target slope and solve.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y^{2} - x = 2 \;\Longrightarrow\; x = y^{2} - 2.} \]
Every segment crossing the parabola $x = y^{2} - 2$ has slope $2$.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 3.}\quad Substitute, keeping the leading sign:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{f(3,4) = -\frac{3}{4}.} \]
The integral curves satisfy $y\,dy = -x\,dx$, so
$\textcolor{workpurple}{\frac{y^2}{2} = -\frac{x^2}{2} + C_1}$, that is
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{x^{2} + y^{2} = C} \qquad\text{(concentric circles).} \]

\noindent\textbf{Solution 4.}\quad Equilibria need both derivatives zero:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y = 0 \text{ and } -x = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; (0,0).} \]
The coefficient matrix
$\left(\begin{smallmatrix}0&1\\-1&0\end{smallmatrix}\right)$ has characteristic
equation $\lambda^2 + 1 = 0$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda = \pm i \;\;(\text{purely imaginary}).} \]
Purely imaginary eigenvalues give a \textcolor{workpurple}{center}: trajectories
are closed orbits circling the origin.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 5.}\quad The system is decoupled, so the coefficient
matrix $\left(\begin{smallmatrix}2&0\\0&3\end{smallmatrix}\right)$ is diagonal
and its eigenvalues sit on the diagonal:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda_1 = 2, \quad \lambda_2 = 3.} \]
Both are real and positive, so the origin is an
\textcolor{workpurple}{unstable node (a source)} --- every nearby trajectory
flows outward.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 6.}\quad Locate where $f$ and $\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}$
lose continuity. With $f = \dfrac{x}{y-2}$,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = -\frac{x}{(y-2)^2},} \]
and both blow up when the denominator vanishes:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y - 2 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; y = 2.} \]
The theorem fails to guarantee uniqueness along the line $y = 2$; everywhere
else a unique solution exists.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 7.}\quad Here $f = \sqrt{y}$ is continuous at $y=0$,
but
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{y}}} \]
is \emph{not} continuous at $y = 0$ --- it blows up. So uniqueness is not
guaranteed, and indeed two solutions pass through the origin:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y = 0} \qquad\text{and}\qquad
   \textcolor{workpurple}{y = \frac{x^{2}}{4}} \quad (x \ge 0), \]
since $\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{x^2}{4}\right) = \frac{x}{2} = \sqrt{x^2/4}$.
The failed hypothesis is continuity of $\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}$.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 8.}\quad Solve $g(x) = x$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{x^{2} = x \;\Longrightarrow\; x(x-1) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x = 0,\ 1.} \]
Classify with $|g'(x)| = |2x|$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{|g'(0)| = 0 < 1 \ (\text{attracting}), \qquad |g'(1)| = 2 > 1 \ (\text{repelling}).} \]

\noindent\textbf{Solution 9.}\quad A bound $|g'(x)| \le k < 1$ on an interval
makes $g$ a contraction there. With $g(x) = \cos x$,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{|g'(x)| = |\sin x|,} \]
which increases on $[0,1]$ and so is largest at $x = 1$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{|\sin x| \le \sin 1 \approx 0.841 < 1.} \]
Thus $g$ is a contraction with $k = \sin 1$. By Banach, $g$ has a
\textcolor{workpurple}{unique} fixed point in $[0,1]$, and iterating cosine from
\emph{any} start converges to it.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 10.}\quad Use $\varphi_{n+1}(x) = 1 + \int_0^x \varphi_n(t)\,dt$
from $\varphi_0 = 1$.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\varphi_1 = 1 + \int_0^x 1\,dt = 1 + x.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\varphi_2 = 1 + \int_0^x (1+t)\,dt = 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\varphi_3 = 1 + \int_0^x \left(1 + t + \frac{t^2}{2}\right)dt = 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2} + \frac{x^3}{6}.} \]
These are the partial sums of the exponential series, so
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\varphi_n \to 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2} + \frac{x^3}{6} + \cdots = e^{x},} \]
the true solution of $y' = y$, $y(0) = 1$.

takeaway.title: Unit 3 Key Takeaway
takeaway.body:
This unit reads differential equations as \textcolor{accentorange}{geometry and
guarantees} rather than formulas. A \textcolor{accentorange}{slope field} turns
$y'=f(x,y)$ into a picture its integral curves follow; the
\textcolor{accentorange}{phase plane} does the same for systems. The
\textcolor{accentorange}{existence and uniqueness theorem} ($f$ and
$\partial f/\partial y$ continuous) says exactly one curve threads each point,
and \textcolor{accentorange}{Picard iteration via the Banach contraction
principle} is the machine that proves it.
